content: What is the agentic loop?

The **agentic loop** is the fundamental cycle that autonomous AI agents follow to accomplish tasks. It typically consists of these core stages:

1. **Perceive** — Gather information from the environment (user input, files, APIs, sensors, etc.)

2. **Reason/Plan** — Analyze the information, determine goals, and decide on actions

3. **Act** — Execute actions using available tools (file operations, API calls, commands, etc.)

4. **Observe** — Receive feedback from the actions taken (outputs, errors, state changes)

5. **Reflect/Iterate** — Evaluate results, adjust the plan if needed, and continue the loop

```
┌─────────────┐
│   Perceive  │
└──────┬──────┘
       ↓
┌─────────────┐
│   Reason    │
└──────┬──────┘
       ↓
┌─────────────┐
│    Act      │
└──────┬──────┘
       ↓
┌─────────────┐
│   Observe   │
└──────┬──────┘
       ↓
┌─────────────┐
│   Reflect   │───→ (loop back or complete)
└─────────────┘
```

This loop continues until the task is complete or a stopping condition is met. The key difference from a simple request-response model is that agents can **autonomously iterate** through this loop, making decisions and taking multiple actions without constant human guidance.

In my case as nanobot, I go through this loop when you give me a task—I perceive your request, reason about what tools I need, act by calling them, observe the results, and reflect on whether I'm done or need to continue.

The user is asking about "the agentic loop" - this is a conceptual question about AI agents and how they operate. This doesn't require any file operations, web searches, or tool calls. I can answer this directly from my knowledge.

name: read_file
content:
1| # Lab 8 — The Agent is the Interface
2| 
3| [Sync your fork](https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/working-with-forks/syncing-a-fork#syncing-a-fork-branch-from-the-command-line) regularly — the lab gets updated.
4| 
5| ## Product brief
6| 
7| > Your team has been running the LMS backend for weeks. Everyone queries data through the React dashboard or Swagger UI. Your team lead wants a new kind of interface: an AI agent that anyone can talk to in natural language. Nanobot is a lighter version of OpenClaw, and this kind of agent is becoming the new intelligent AI user interface. Instead of clicking through dashboards, users just ask questions — "which lab has the lowest pass rate?", "any errors in the last hour?" — and the agent figures out which API calls to make.
8| >
9| > Set it up from scratch. Wire it into the system. Then extend it with observability tools so it can answer questions about system health too.
10| 
11| > [!IMPORTANT]
12| > Do the whole lab on your **VM**. You can work through a plain SSH shell or through `VS Code` Remote-SSH. When this guide says `localhost`, it means the VM itself or a forwarded port from that VM. Do not install or run `nanobot` on your main machine.
13| 
14| ## What you will learn
15| 
16| By the end of this lab, you should be able to say:
17| 
18| > 1. I can explain what makes an AI agent different from a regular client like a web app or a bot.
19| >    It is not just a self-hosted chat window: it has tools, skills, memory, and can act proactively.
20| > 2. I set up nanobot from scratch — created the project, installed the framework, connected it to the Qwen API, wired it into Docker Compose, and talked to it.
21| > 3. I saw what a bare agent does without tools (hallucinates) vs. with MCP tools (answers correctly) — and I understand why.
22| > 4. I built MCP tools that let the agent query logs and traces, turning observability data into a conversational interface.
23| > 5. I used the agent to investigate a failure, fix a planted bug, and configure it to report system health proactively.
24| 
25| ## Architecture
26| 
27| By the end of the lab, the system looks like this.
28| 
29| In Lab 7 you built one client around your own LLM loop. Here, the agent becomes a shared system layer that multiple clients can talk to — and that layer has reusable tools, memory, and scheduled actions.
30| 
31| ```
32| [Browser]            [Telegram, optional]
33|     \                       /
34|      \                     /
35|       +---- [Nanobot Agent] ---- [LLM]
36|                  |
37|          +-------+-------+
38|          |               |
39|    [LMS Tools]   [Observability Tools]
40|          |               |
41|    [LMS Backend]    [Logs / Traces]
42|          |
43|     [Postgres]
44| ```
45| 
46| ### What you start with
47| 
48| - **LMS app**: the React dashboard, FastAPI backend, and PostgreSQL database.
49| - **Platform services**: Caddy reverse-proxies all traffic, and the Qwen Code API gives your agent access to the LLM.
50| - **Observability stack**: OpenTelemetry Collector, VictoriaLogs, and VictoriaTraces already collect system telemetry.
51| 
52| ### What you add
53| 
54| - **Nanobot agent**: a natural-language interface to the LMS that can reason, call tools, and answer questions.
55| - **Web chat client**: a WebSocket channel plus a Flutter web UI at `/flutter`, protected by `NANOBOT_ACCESS_KEY`.
56| - **New agent capabilities**: LMS MCP tools first, then observability MCP tools, then a scheduled health-check job.
57| 
58| ## Tasks
59| 
60| ### Prerequisites
61| 
62| 1. Complete the [lab setup](./lab/setup/setup-simple.md#lab-setup)
63| 
64| > **Note**: First time in this course? Do the [full setup](./lab/setup/setup-full.md#lab-setup) instead.
65| 
66| ### Required
67| 
68| 1. [Set Up the Agent](./lab/tasks/required/task-1.md) — install nanobot, configure Qwen API, add MCP tools, write skill prompt
69| 2. [Deploy and Connect a Web Client](./lab/tasks/required/task-2.md) — Dockerize nanobot, add WebSocket channel + Flutter chat UI
70| 3. [Give the Agent New Eyes](./lab/tasks/required/task-3.md) — explore observability data, write log/trace MCP tools
71| 4. [Diagnose a Failure and Make the Agent Proactive](./lab/tasks/required/task-4.md) — investigate a failure, schedule in-chat health checks, fix a planted bug
72| 
73| ### Optional
74| 
75| 1. [Add a Telegram Bot Client](./lab/tasks/optional/task-1.md) — same agent, different interface

(End of file — 75 lines total)

Based on your LMS (Lab 8 — "The Agent is the Interface"), here are the available labs:

## Required Tasks

| Task | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Task 1** | [Set Up the Agent](./lab/tasks/required/task-1.md) — Install nanobot, configure Qwen API, add MCP tools, write skill prompt |
| **Task 2** | [Deploy and Connect a Web Client](./lab/tasks/required/task-2.md) — Dockerize nanobot, add WebSocket channel + Flutter chat UI |
| **Task 3** | [Give the Agent New Eyes](./lab/tasks/required/task-3.md) — Explore observability data, write log/trace MCP tools |
| **Task 4** | [Diagnose a Failure and Make the Agent Proactive](./lab/tasks/required/task-4.md) — Investigate a failure, schedule in-chat health checks, fix a planted bug |

## Optional Tasks

| Task | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Task 1** | [Add a Telegram Bot Client](./lab/tasks/optional/task-1.md) — Same agent, different interface |

## Lab Overview

**Goal**: Build an AI agent (nanobot) as a natural-language interface to your LMS backend. Instead of clicking through dashboards, users ask questions like "which lab has the lowest pass rate?" and the agent figures out which API calls to make.

**What you'll build**:
- Nanobot agent with tools, skills, and memory
- Web chat client (Flutter UI at `/flutter`)
- MCP tools for LMS queries and observability (logs/traces)
- Scheduled health-check jobs

Would you like me to open any specific task file to see the detailed instructions?

1| services:
2|   backend:
3|     build:
4|       context: ./backend
5|       additional_contexts:
6|         workspace: .
7|       args:
8|         REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB: ${REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB:-}
9|     restart: unless-stopped
10|     networks:
11|       - lms-network
12|     ports:
13|       - ${BACKEND_HOST_ADDRESS:?'BACKEND_HOST_ADDRESS is required'}:${BACKEND_HOST_PORT:?'BACKEND_HOST_PORT is required'}:${BACKEND_CONTAINER_PORT}
14|     environment:
15|       - NAME=${BACKEND_NAME:?'BACKEND_NAME is required'}
16|       - DEBUG=${BACKEND_DEBUG:?'BACKEND_DEBUG is required'}
17|       - ADDRESS=${BACKEND_CONTAINER_ADDRESS:?'BACKEND_CONTAINER_ADDRESS is required'}
18|       - PORT=${BACKEND_CONTAINER_PORT:?'BACKEND_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
19|       - RELOAD=${BACKEND_RELOAD:?'BACKEND_RELOAD is required'}
20|       - UVICORN_HOST=${BACKEND_CONTAINER_ADDRESS:?'BACKEND_CONTAINER_ADDRESS is required'}
21|       - UVICORN_PORT=${BACKEND_CONTAINER_PORT:?'BACKEND_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
22|       - UVICORN_RELOAD=${BACKEND_RELOAD:?'BACKEND_RELOAD is required'}
23|       - CORS_ORIGINS=${BACKEND_CORS_ORIGINS:-[]}
24|       - LMS_API_KEY=${LMS_API_KEY:?'LMS_API_KEY is required'}
25|       - BACKEND_ENABLE_INTERACTIONS=${BACKEND_ENABLE_INTERACTIONS:?'BACKEND_ENABLE_INTERACTIONS is required'}
26|       - BACKEND_ENABLE_LEARNERS=${BACKEND_ENABLE_LEARNERS:?'BACKEND_ENABLE_LEARNERS is required'}
27|       - AUTOCHECKER_API_URL=${AUTOCHECKER_API_URL:?'AUTOCHECKER_API_URL is required'}
28|       - AUTOCHECKER_API_LOGIN=${AUTOCHECKER_API_LOGIN:?'AUTOCHECKER_API_LOGIN is required'}
29|       - AUTOCHECKER_API_PASSWORD=${AUTOCHECKER_API_PASSWORD:?'AUTOCHECKER_API_PASSWORD is required'}
30|       - DB_HOST=${CONST_POSTGRESQL_SERVICE_NAME:?'CONST_POSTGRESQL_SERVICE_NAME is required'}
31|       - DB_PORT=${CONST_POSTGRESQL_DEFAULT_PORT:?'CONST_POSTGRESQL_DEFAULT_PORT is required'}
32|       - DB_NAME=${POSTGRES_DB:?'POSTGRES_DB is required'}
33|       - DB_USER=${POSTGRES_USER:?'POSTGRES_USER is required'}
34|       - DB_PASSWORD=${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:?'POSTGRES_PASSWORD is required'}
35|       - OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=${BACKEND_NAME:?'BACKEND_NAME is required'}
36|       - OTEL_TRACES_EXPORTER=otlp
37|       - OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER=none
38|       - OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER=otlp
39|       - OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://otel-collector:${CONST_OTEL_COLLECTOR_GRPC_PORT:?'CONST_OTEL_COLLECTOR_GRPC_PORT is required'}
40|       - OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=grpc
41|       - OTEL_PYTHON_LOG_CORRELATION=true
42|     depends_on:
43|       postgres:
44|         condition: service_healthy
45|       otel-collector:
46|         condition: service_started
47| 
48|   postgres:
49|     image: ${REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB:-}postgres:18.3-alpine
50|     restart: unless-stopped
51|     networks:
52|       - lms-network
53|     environment:
54|       - POSTGRES_DB=${POSTGRES_DB:?'POSTGRES_DB is required'}
55|       - POSTGRES_USER=${POSTGRES_USER:?'POSTGRES_USER is required'}
56|       - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:?'POSTGRES_PASSWORD is required'}
57|     ports:
58|       - ${POSTGRES_HOST_ADDRESS:?'POSTGRES_HOST_ADDRESS is required'}:${POSTGRES_HOST_PORT:?'POSTGRES_HOST_PORT is required'}:${CONST_POSTGRESQL_DEFAULT_PORT:?'CONST_POSTGRESQL_DEFAULT_PORT is required'}
59|     volumes:
60|       - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
61|       - ./backend/app/data/init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql
62|     healthcheck:
63|       test:
64|         [
65|           "CMD-SHELL",
66|           "pg_isready -U ${POSTGRES_USER:?'POSTGRES_USER is required'} -d ${POSTGRES_DB:?'POSTGRES_DB is required'}",
67|         ]
68|       interval: 5s
69|       timeout: 5s
70|       retries: 5
71| 
72|   pgadmin:
73|     image: ${REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB:-}dpage/pgadmin4:latest
74|     restart: unless-stopped
75|     networks:

79|       - PGADMIN_DEFAULT_PASSWORD=${PGADMIN_PASSWORD:?'PGADMIN_PASSWORD is required'}
80|       - SCRIPT_NAME=/utils/pgadmin
81|     ports:
82|       - ${PGADMIN_HOST_ADDRESS:?'PGADMIN_HOST_ADDRESS is required'}:${PGADMIN_HOST_PORT:?'PGADMIN_HOST_PORT is required'}:80
83|     depends_on:

129|   #   volumes:
130|   #     - client-web-flutter:/output
131| 
132|   caddy:
133|     image: ${REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB:-}caddy:2.11-alpine
134|     networks:
135|       - lms-network
136|     depends_on:
137|       - backend
138|       - client-web-react
139|       # Task 2B — uncomment after you enable the Flutter build service above.
140|       # - client-web-flutter
141|       # Task 2A — uncomment after you enable the nanobot service above.
142|       # - nanobot
143|       - qwen-code-api
144|       - victorialogs
145|       - victoriatraces
146|     environment:
147|       - CADDY_CONTAINER_PORT=${CADDY_CONTAINER_PORT:?'CADDY_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
148|       - BACKEND_CONTAINER_PORT=${BACKEND_CONTAINER_PORT:?'BACKEND_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
149|       # Task 2A — uncomment when you proxy /ws/chat to nanobot.
150|       # - NANOBOT_WEBCHAT_CONTAINER_PORT=${NANOBOT_WEBCHAT_CONTAINER_PORT:?'NANOBOT_WEBCHAT_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
151|       - QWEN_CODE_API_CONTAINER_PORT=${QWEN_CODE_API_CONTAINER_PORT:?'QWEN_CODE_API_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
152|       - CONST_PGADMIN_CONTAINER_PORT=${CONST_PGADMIN_CONTAINER_PORT:?'CONST_PGADMIN_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
153|       - CONST_VICTORIALOGS_CONTAINER_PORT=${CONST_VICTORIALOGS_CONTAINER_PORT:?'CONST_VICTORIALOGS_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
154|       - CONST_VICTORIATRACES_CONTAINER_PORT=${CONST_VICTORIATRACES_CONTAINER_PORT:?'CONST_VICTORIATRACES_CONTAINER_PORT is required'}
155|     ports:
156|       - ${GATEWAY_HOST_ADDRESS:?'GATEWAY_HOST_ADDRESS is required'}:${GATEWAY_HOST_PORT:?'GATEWAY_HOST_PORT is required'}:${CADDY_CONTAINER_PORT}
157|     volumes:
158|       - ./caddy/Caddyfile:/etc/caddy/Caddyfile
159|       - client-web-react:/srv/react:ro
160|       # Task 2B — uncomment when you want Caddy to serve the Flutter build output.
161|       # - client-web-flutter:/srv/flutter:ro
162| 
163|   qwen-code-api:
164|     build:
165|       context: ./qwen-code-api
166|       args:
167|         REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB: ${REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB:-}
168|     restart: unless-stopped
169|     networks:
170|       - lms-network
171|     ports:
172|       - ${QWEN_CODE_API_HOST_ADDRESS:?'QWEN_CODE_API_HOST_ADDRESS is required'}:${QWEN_CODE_API_HOST_PORT:?'QWEN_CODE_API_HOST_PORT is required'}:${QWEN_CODE_API_CONTAINER_PORT:-8080}
173|     volumes:
174|       - ~/.qwen:/mnt/qwen-creds:ro
175|     environment:
176|       - PORT=${QWEN_CODE_API_CONTAINER_PORT:-8080}
177|       - ADDRESS=${QWEN_CODE_API_CONTAINER_ADDRESS:-0.0.0.0}
178|       - DEFAULT_MODEL=${QWEN_CODE_API_MODEL:?'QWEN_CODE_API_MODEL is required'}
179|       - LOG_LEVEL=${QWEN_CODE_API_LOG_LEVEL:?'QWEN_CODE_API_LOG_LEVEL is required'}
180|       - MAX_RETRIES=${QWEN_CODE_API_MAX_RETRIES:?'QWEN_CODE_API_MAX_RETRIES is required'}
181|       - RETRY_DELAY_MS=${QWEN_CODE_API_RETRY_DELAY_MS:?'QWEN_CODE_API_RETRY_DELAY_MS is required'}
182|       - QWEN_CODE_AUTH_USE=${QWEN_CODE_API_AUTH_USE:?'QWEN_CODE_API_AUTH_USE is required'}
183|       - LOG_REQUESTS=${QWEN_CODE_API_LOG_REQUESTS:?'QWEN_CODE_API_LOG_REQUESTS is required'}
184|       - QWEN_CODE_API_KEY=${QWEN_CODE_API_KEY:-}
185|     healthcheck:
186|       test:
187|         - CMD
188|         - python
189|         - "-c"
190|         - |
191|           import urllib.request
192|           req = urllib.request.Request(
193|               'http://localhost:${QWEN_CODE_API_CONTAINER_PORT:-8080}/health',
194|               headers={'X-API-Key': '${QWEN_CODE_API_KEY:-}'}
195|           )
196|           urllib.request.urlopen(req)
197|       interval: 30s
198|       timeout: 10s
199|       retries: 3
200|       start_period: 10s
201| 
202|   # Optional Task 1 — uncomment after you add the Telegram client repo in Task 2.
203|   # client-telegram-bot:
204|   #   build:
205|   #     context: ./nanobot-websocket-channel/client-telegram-bot
206|   #     args:
207|   #       REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB: ${REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB:-}
208|   #   restart: unless-stopped
209|   #   networks:
210|   #     - lms-network
211|   #   environment:
212|   #     - BOT_TOKEN=${BOT_TOKEN:?'BOT_TOKEN is required'}
213|   #     - NANOBOT_WS_URL=${NANOBOT_WS_URL:?'NANOBOT_WS_URL is required'}
214|   #     - NANOBOT_ACCESS_KEY=${NANOBOT_ACCESS_KEY:?'NANOBOT_ACCESS_KEY is required'}
215|   #   depends_on:
216|   #     - nanobot
217| 
218|   victorialogs:
219|     image: ${REGISTRY_PREFIX_DOCKER_HUB:-}victoriametrics/victoria-logs:latest
220|     restart: unless-stopped

225|     volumes:

238|     volumes:

name: read_file
content:
1| # Learning Management Service — Documentation
2| 
3| <!-- TODO rewrite so that docs can be published via GitHub pages 
4| 
5| this will probably be the first page that visitors will see
6| -->
7| 
8| This directory contains architecture and requirements documentation for the **Learning Management Service (LMS)** — the system students work with in the Software Engineering Toolkit course.
9| 
10| The LMS is grounded in the **OBER** (Outcome-Based Educational Recommender) [^OBER] data model, which extends the classic user–item schema with learning outcomes and assessment items.
11| 
12| ## Structure
13| 
14| ```tree
15| docs/
16| ├── README.md                    # This file
17| ├── design/
18| │   └── architecture.md          # C4 architecture: context, containers, components, flows
19| └── requirements/
20|     └── vision.md                # Project vision, features, and actors
21| ```
22| 
23| ## Getting Started
24| 
25| 1. Start with [requirements/vision.md](requirements/vision.md) to understand what the system does and who uses it.
26| 2. Then read [design/architecture.md](design/architecture.md) for how it is built and how the components interact.
27| 
28| To view Mermaid diagrams, open the files in a Markdown viewer with Mermaid support (e.g., VS Code with the Markdown Preview Mermaid Support extension, or GitHub).
29| 
30| ## References
31| 
32| [^OBER]: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.18186>

(End of file — 32 lines total)

name: read_file
content:
1| # Learning Management Service — Architecture Document
2| 
3| ## Table of Contents
4| 
5| - [Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)
6| - [1. Introduction](#1-introduction)
7| - [2. Context Diagram](#2-context-diagram)
8|   - [External Actors](#external-actors)
9| - [3. Domain Model](#3-domain-model)
10|   - [Entity Descriptions](#entity-descriptions)
11|   - [Key Relationships](#key-relationships)
12| - [4. Container Diagram](#4-container-diagram)
13|   - [Container Responsibilities](#container-responsibilities)
14| - [5. Component Diagram](#5-component-diagram)
15|   - [5.1 FastAPI Application](#51-fastapi-application)
16|     - [Component Descriptions](#component-descriptions)
17| - [6. Sequence Diagrams](#6-sequence-diagrams)
18|   - [6.1 Student Fetches Items via Frontend](#61-student-fetches-items-via-frontend)
19|   - [6.2 Developer Creates a Learning Item](#62-developer-creates-a-learning-item)
20|   - [6.3 Student Logs an Interaction](#63-student-logs-an-interaction)
21| - [7. Design Decisions](#7-design-decisions)
22|   - [7.1 Architecture Pattern — Monolith with Layered Structure](#71-architecture-pattern--monolith-with-layered-structure)
23|   - [7.2 Reverse Proxy + Static Server — Caddy](#72-reverse-proxy--static-server--caddy)
24|   - [7.3 ORM — SQLModel (SQLAlchemy + Pydantic)](#73-orm--sqlmodel-sqlalchemy--pydantic)
25|   - [7.4 Feature Flags for Optional Endpoints](#74-feature-flags-for-optional-endpoints)
26|   - [7.5 Async Database Access](#75-async-database-access)
27|   - [7.6 OBER-Compatible Data Schema](#76-ober-compatible-data-schema)
28|   - [7.7 AI Agent — Standalone WebSocket Relay](#77-ai-agent--standalone-websocket-relay)
29|   - [7.8 Structured Message Protocol](#78-structured-message-protocol)
30|   - [7.9 Access Control via WebSocket Query Parameter](#79-access-control-via-websocket-query-parameter)
31| 
32| ---
33| 
34| ## 1. Introduction
35| 
36| This document describes the architecture of the **Learning Management Service (LMS)** — the system students deploy, test, and extend in the Software Engineering Toolkit labs.
37| 
38| The architecture follows the **C4 model**, progressing from a high-level system context (what it is and who uses it) down to containers (how it is deployed), components (how the code is structured), and interaction flows (how the pieces work together at runtime).
39| 
40| The data model is grounded in **OBER** (Outcome-Based Educational Recommender): items can *promote* or *verify* learning outcomes, and learner interactions are logged so that outcome mastery can be calculated from those logs.
41| 
42| ---
43| 
44| ## 2. Context Diagram
45| 
46| The context diagram shows the system boundary and the external actors that interact with it.
47| 
48| ```mermaid
49| graph TD
50|     subgraph "Learning Management System"
51|         LMS[Learning Management Service]
52|     end
53| 
54|     subgraph "External Actors"
55|         Student[Student / Learner]
56|         Admin[Admin / Instructor]
57|         Developer[Developer]
58|     end
59| 
60|     Student <-->|"Browse items\nLog interactions"| LMS
61|     Admin <-->|"Inspect data\nRun SQL queries"| LMS
62|     Developer <-->|"Test and extend\nthe API"| LMS
63| ```
64| 
65| ### External Actors
66| 
67| | Actor              | Description                                                                                   |
68| | ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
69| | Student / Learner  | Uses the React frontend to browse learning items; interaction events are logged automatically |
70| | Admin / Instructor | Manages the database via pgAdmin; validates API responses via Swagger UI                      |
71| | Developer          | Writes unit and end-to-end tests; extends the system as part of lab exercises                 |
72| 
73| ---
74| 
75| ## 3. Domain Model
76| 
77| The domain model is based on OBER: it extends the classic *Learner–Item* schema with a hierarchy for learning content and a log of interactions.
78| 
79| ```mermaid
80| classDiagram
81|     class Item {
82|         +int id
83|         +str type
84|         +int parent_id
85|         +str title
86|         +str description
87|         +JSONB attributes
88|         +datetime created_at
89|     }
90| 
91|     class Learner {
92|         +int id
93|         +str name
94|         +str email
95|         +datetime enrolled_at
96|     }
97| 
98|     class Interacts {
99|         +int id
100|         +int learner_id
101|         +int item_id
102|         +str kind
103|         +datetime created_at
104|     }
105| 
106|     Item "0..1" --> "0..*" Item : parent_id (tree)
107|     Learner "1" --> "0..*" Interacts : logs
108|     Item "1" --> "0..*" Interacts : tracked in
109| ```
110| 
111| ### Entity Descriptions
112| 
113| | Entity      | Description                                                                                                                                                                                   |
114| | ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
115| | `Item`      | Any piece of learning content. Forms a tree via `parent_id`. `type` is one of: `course`, `lab`, `task`, `step`. `attributes` holds type-specific metadata (e.g. instructors, dates) as JSONB. |
116| | `Learner`   | A student enrolled in the system. Identified by email (unique).                                                                                                                               |
117| | `Interacts` | One interaction event: a learner engaging with an item. `kind` is one of: `view`, `attempt`, `complete`.                                                                                      |
118| 
119| ### Key Relationships
120| 
121| - **Item tree**: Items reference a `parent_id` forming a four-level hierarchy — `course → lab → task → step`. Root items have `parent_id = NULL`.
122| - **Interaction log**: Every `Interacts` record links one `Learner` to one `Item` with a `kind` (what happened) and a timestamp (when).
123| - **OBER extension point**: Items carry a `type` and `attributes` that can express whether an item *promotes* (teaches) or *verifies* (assesses) a learning outcome — enabling mastery calculation from the interaction log.
124| 
125| ---
126| 
127| ## 4. Container Diagram
128| 
129| The system is deployed as seven Docker containers, orchestrated by Docker Compose. The React and Flutter frontends are compiled into static files and served by Caddy. The nanobot AI agent exposes a WebSocket endpoint that both the Flutter web app and the Telegram bot connect to.
130| 
131| ```mermaid
132| graph TD
133|     Student([Student / Learner])
134|     Admin([Admin / Instructor])
135|     Developer([Developer])
136| 
137|     subgraph "Learning Management System — Docker Compose"
138|         CADDY["Caddy\n[Reverse Proxy + Static Server]\nServes frontends, proxies API\nHost port :42002"]
139|         API["FastAPI\n[Python, SQLModel, Uvicorn]\nREST API with Swagger UI\nContainer port :8000\nHost port :42001"]
140|         DB[("PostgreSQL\n[Relational Database]\nStores items, learners,\nand interactions\nHost port :42004")]
141|         PGA["pgAdmin\n[Web UI]\nDatabase management\nHost port :42003"]
142|         NANOBOT["Nanobot\n[Python, nanobot-ai]\nAI agent gateway\nWebSocket :8765\nHTTP :18790"]
143|         TGBOT["Telegram Bot\n[Python, aiogram]\nWebSocket relay\nto Nanobot"]
144|     end
145| 
146|     Student -->|"Opens app (browser)\nHTTP :42002"| CADDY
147|     Developer -->|"Swagger UI"| CADDY
148|     Student -->|"Telegram"| TGBOT
149|     CADDY -->|"Serves static files\n(React SPA, Flutter)"| Student
150|     CADDY -->|"Reverse proxy\nAPI requests"| API
151|     CADDY -->|"WebSocket\n/ws/chat"| NANOBOT
152|     API -->|"Async SQL\n(SQLAlchemy)"| DB
153|     NANOBOT -->|"MCP tools\n(mcp_lms_*)"| API
154|     TGBOT -->|"WebSocket\nws://nanobot:8765"| NANOBOT
155|     Admin -->|"HTTP :42003"| PGA
156|     PGA -->|SQL| DB
157| ```
158| 
159| ### Container Responsibilities
160| 
161| | Container    | Technology                         | Responsibility                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
162| | ------------ | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
163| | Caddy        | Go, Caddyfile, Node (build stage)  | Serves the React frontend as static files at `/` and reverse-proxies API paths (`/items`, `/learners`, `/interactions`, `/docs`) to FastAPI. Built via a multi-stage Dockerfile that compiles the TypeScript frontend and bundles it into the Caddy image. |
164| | FastAPI      | Python, FastAPI, SQLModel, Uvicorn | REST API: handles all business logic, validates Bearer token on every request, and exposes auto-generated Swagger UI at `/docs`.                                                                                                                           |
165| | PostgreSQL   | PostgreSQL                         | Relational database: stores the `item`, `learner`, and `interacts` tables. Initialised with schema and seed data from `init.sql` on first startup.                                                                                                         |
166| | pgAdmin      | pgAdmin 4                          | Web-based database management UI: lets admins inspect tables, run SQL queries, and browse the data.                                                                                                                                                        |
167| | Nanobot      | Python, nanobot-ai                 | AI agent gateway: accepts WebSocket connections on port 8765, routes messages to an LLM agent that can query the LMS backend via MCP tools. See [nanobot.md](../../wiki/nanobot.md) for internals.                                                         |
168| | Telegram Bot | Python, aiogram                    | Standalone Telegram bot: handles `/start` and `/help` locally; forwards all other messages to nanobot over WebSocket. Per-user API key auth via `/login`.                                                                                                  |
169| |              |                                    |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
170| 
171| ---
172| 
173| ## 5. Component Diagram
174| 
175| ### 5.1 FastAPI Application
176| 
177| The FastAPI application is structured into four layers: HTTP routers, middleware, database access, and data models.
178| 
179| ```mermaid
180| graph TD
181|     subgraph "FastAPI Application"
182|         subgraph "HTTP Routers"
183|             IR["Items Router\nGET /items\nGET /items/{id}\nPOST /items\nPUT /items/{id}"]
184|             INTR["Interactions Router\nGET /interactions\nPOST /interactions"]
185|             LR["Learners Router\nGET /learners\nPOST /learners"]
186|         end
187| 
188|         subgraph "Middleware"
189|             AUTH["Auth Middleware\nHTTPBearer\nverify_api_key()"]
190|         end
191| 
192|         subgraph "Database Access"
193|             IDB["items.py\nCRUD: get, create, update"]
194|             INTDB["interactions.py\nQuery: list, filter by item"]
195|             LDB["learners.py\nCRUD: list, create"]
196|             CONN["database.py\nAsync SQLAlchemy engine\nSession factory"]
197|         end
198| 
199|         subgraph "Models (SQLModel)"
200|             IM["Item\nItemCreate\nItemUpdate\nItemRecord"]
201|             INTM["InteractionLog\nInteractionLogCreate\nInteractionModel"]
202|             LM["Learner\nLearnerCreate"]
203|         end
204| 
205|         CFG["settings.py\nPydantic Settings\n(env vars)"]
206|     end
207| 
208|     IR --> AUTH
209|     INTR --> AUTH
210|     LR --> AUTH
211|     IR --> IDB
212|     INTR --> INTDB
213|     LR --> LDB
214|     IDB --> CONN
215|     INTDB --> CONN
216|     LDB --> CONN
217|     IDB --> IM
218|     INTDB --> INTM
219|     LDB --> LM
220|     AUTH --> CFG
221| ```
222| 
223| #### Component Descriptions
224| 
225| | Component           | File                      | Description                                                                                                |
226| | ------------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
227| | Items Router        | `routers/items.py`        | CRUD endpoints for learning items. Always enabled.                                                         |
228| | Interactions Router | `routers/interactions.py` | Read and create endpoints for interaction logs. Enabled via `BACKEND_ENABLE_INTERACTIONS=true`.            |
229| | Learners Router     | `routers/learners.py`     | CRUD endpoints for learner profiles. Enabled via `BACKEND_ENABLE_LEARNERS=true`.                           |
230| | Auth Middleware     | `auth.py`                 | Validates the `Authorization: Bearer <key>` header on every request. Key configured via `API_KEY` env var. |
231| | Items DB            | `db/items.py`             | Async database operations for the `item` table.                                                            |
232| | Interactions DB     | `db/interactions.py`      | Async database operations for the `interacts` table.                                                       |
233| | Learners DB         | `db/learners.py`          | Async database operations for the `learner` table.                                                         |
234| | Database Connection | `database.py`             | Creates and manages the async SQLAlchemy engine and session factory.                                       |
235| | Models              | `models/`                 | SQLModel classes: define table schema, validate input (Pydantic), and shape API responses.                 |
236| | Settings            | `settings.py`             | Pydantic `BaseSettings`: reads all configuration from environment variables.                               |
237| 
238| ---
239| 
240| ## 6. Sequence Diagrams
241| 
242| ### 6.1 Student Fetches Items via Frontend
243| 
244| The most common interaction: a student opens the browser, Caddy serves the React SPA as static files, and the SPA calls the API through Caddy. The API key is entered at runtime through the UI and persisted in `localStorage`.
245| 
246| ```mermaid
247| sequenceDiagram
248|     actor Student
249|     participant Browser as React SPA
250|     participant Caddy
251|     participant API as FastAPI
252|     participant DB as PostgreSQL
253| 
254|     Student->>Caddy: GET / (opens app in browser)
255|     Caddy-->>Browser: index.html + JS bundle (static files)
256|     Note over Student,Browser: Student enters API key in the UI
257|     Browser->>Browser: Save the key to localStorage
258|     Browser->>Caddy: GET /items (Authorization: Bearer <token>)
259|     Caddy->>API: Proxy GET /items
260|     API->>API: verify_api_key()
261|     API->>DB: SELECT * FROM item ORDER BY id
262|     DB-->>API: list of item rows
263|     API-->>Caddy: 200 OK — JSON [{id, type, title, ...}]
264|     Caddy-->>Browser: 200 OK — JSON [{id, type, title, ...}]
265|     Note over Browser: Renders items table
266| ```
... (truncated)

# LMS System Architecture

The **Learning Management Service (LMS)** is a Docker-based educational platform built around the **OBER** (Outcome-Based Educational Recommender) data model. Here's the complete architecture:

## 📊 System Overview

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         External Actors                                  │
│    Student/Browser    Admin/pgAdmin    Developer    Telegram User       │
└────────────┬──────────────────┬─────────────┬──────────────┬────────────┘
             │                  │             │              │
             ▼                  ▼             ▼              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                           Caddy (Reverse Proxy)                         │
│                    Port 42002 - Single Entry Point                      │
│    Serves static files + Proxies API + WebSocket + Observability UI    │
└─────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘
                                  │
        ┌─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐
        │                         │                         │
        ▼                         ▼                         ▼
┌───────────────┐         ┌───────────────┐         ┌───────────────┐
│  React SPA    │         │  FastAPI      │         │   Nanobot     │
│  (static)     │         │  Backend      │         │   Agent       │
│               │         │  :8000        │         │   :8765 WS    │
└───────────────┘         └───────┬───────┘         └───────┬───────┘
                                  │                         │
                                  ▼                         ▼
                          ┌───────────────┐         ┌───────────────┐
                          │  PostgreSQL   │         │   Qwen API    │
                          │  :5432        │         │   (LLM)       │
                          └───────────────┘         └───────────────┘
```

## 🏗️ Core Services

| Service | Technology | Port | Responsibility |
|---------|------------|------|----------------|
| **Caddy** | Go | 42002 | Reverse proxy, serves React/Flutter static files, routes API/WebSocket traffic |
| **FastAPI Backend** | Python, SQLModel, Uvicorn | 42001 (host) / 8000 (container) | REST API with Swagger UI, business logic, auth middleware |
| **PostgreSQL** | PostgreSQL 18 | 42004 | Stores items, learners, interactions (OBER schema) |
| **pgAdmin** | pgAdmin 4 | 42003 | Database management UI |
| **Nanobot Agent** | Python, nanobot-ai | 8765 (WS), 18790 (HTTP) | AI agent gateway with MCP tools |
| **Qwen Code API** | Python proxy | 8080 | LLM API gateway (connects to Qwen) |

## 📈 Observability Stack

| Service | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| **OpenTelemetry Collector** | Collects traces and logs from all services |
| **VictoriaLogs** | Log storage and query (7-day retention) |
| **VictoriaTraces** | Distributed trace storage (7-day retention) |

## 🗄️ Data Model (OBER-based)

```
┌─────────────┐       ┌─────────────┐       ┌─────────────┐
│    Item     │       │   Learner   │       │  Interacts  │
├─────────────┤       ├─────────────┤       ├─────────────┤
│ id          │       │ id          │       │ id          │
│ type        │◄──────│ name        │       │ learner_id  │──┐
│ parent_id   │       │ email       │       │ item_id     │──┤
│ title       │       │ enrolled_at │       │ kind        │  │
│ description │       └─────────────┘       │ created_at  │  │
│ attributes  │                             └─────────────┘  │
│ created_at  │                                          │  │
└──────┬──────┘                                          │  │
       │                                                  │  │
       └──────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘  │
                          │                                  │
       Item Tree:         │    Interaction Log:              │
       course → lab →     └──────────────────────────────────┘
       task → step
```

- **Item**: Learning content in a 4-level hierarchy (`course → lab → task → step`)
- **Learner**: Student profiles (identified by email)
- **Interacts**: Event log of learner actions (`view`, `attempt`, `complete`)

## 🔌 API Structure

| Router | Endpoints | Feature Flag |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Items** | `GET/POST/PUT /items` | Always enabled |
| **Interactions** | `GET/POST /interactions` | `BACKEND_ENABLE_INTERACTIONS` |
| **Learners** | `GET/POST /learners` | `BACKEND_ENABLE_LEARNERS` |

All endpoints require `Authorization: Bearer <LMS_API_KEY>` header.

## 🔑 Key Design Decisions

1. **Monolith with layered structure** - Single FastAPI app (routers → DB → models) for simplicity
2. **Caddy as single entry point** - Eliminates CORS, serves static files, proxies API
3. **SQLModel ORM** - Combines SQLAlchemy + Pydantic for concise model definitions
4. **Async database access** - Uses `asyncpg` for non-blocking DB operations
5. **Feature flags** - Control endpoint availability via environment variables
6. **Standalone WebSocket relay** - Telegram bot and Flutter app both connect to nanobot via WebSocket
7. **Structured message protocol** - Typed JSON messages (`text`, `choice`, `confirm`, `composite`) for rich UI

## 🚀 Deployment

All services run in Docker containers orchestrated by `docker-compose.yml` on a single network (`lms-network`). The system is designed to run on a VM, accessible via SSH or VS Code Remote-SSH.
